title: Cytisus triangularis
intro: Cytisus triangularis, also known as Scotch broom, is a perennial legume native to the Mediterranean region, characterized by its yellow flowers and triangular seed pods. The plant has been traditionally used to treat various health conditions, including digestive issues and inflammation, due to its potential health benefits, which include antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and antimicrobial properties. The bioactive constituents of Scotch broom include isoflavones, such as genistein and formononetin, which have been reported to have estrogenic activity and may contribute to its therapeutic effects. The most common herbal preparations made with Cytisus triangularis include teas, capsules, and tinctures, often used to support menopausal health and alleviate symptoms associated with hormonal imbalances.
taxonomy: Cytisus triangularis is classified in the kingdom Plantae, further divided into the clade Angiosperms, the clade Eudicots, the order Fabales, the family Fabaceae, and the genus Cytisus, with the species being Cytisus triangularis itself. It belongs to the subgenus Cytisus. The classification of Cytisus triangularis places it within the flowering plant family Fabaceae. Cytisus triangularis is a part of the Cytisus genus, which includes several other species. The exact classification may be subject to slight variations depending on the source.

side_effects: Cytisus triangularis, also known as the pink rockrose or Scotch broom, contains toxic compounds like cytisine, which can cause a range of side effects in humans and animals. If ingested, it can lead to nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea. In severe cases, it may also cause seizures, hallucinations, and even death.
